questions.question:
Suppose a circular queue of capacity (n-1) elements is implemented with an array of n elements. Assume that the insertion and deletion operations are carried out using REAR and FRONT as array index variables, respectively. Initially, REAR = FRONT = 0. The conditions to detect queue full and queue empty are:

A. full: (REAR+1) mod n == FRONT
   empty: REAR == FRONT
B. full: (REAR+1) mod n == FRONT
   empty: (FRONT+1) mod n == REAR
C. full: REAR == FRONT
   empty: (REAR+1) mod n == FRONT
D. full: (FRONT+1) mod n == REAR
   empty: REAR == FRONT
questions.answer:
A. full: (REAR+1) mod n == FRONT
   empty: REAR == FRONT
questions.to_find: Conditions to detect queue full and queue empty for a circular queue of capacity (n-1) in an array of n elements

understand.plain: We have a circular queue that can hold at most (n-1) elements, stored in an array of size n. REAR is used for insertion and FRONT for deletion, both starting at 0. We need to identify when the queue is full (has n-1 elements) and when it is empty (has 0 elements). One slot is intentionally left unused to distinguish full from empty.

keywords.explain: A queue implemented in a circular manner so that the rear wraps around to the beginning of the array after reaching the end, avoiding wasted space.
keywords.example: Array of size 5, when REAR reaches index 4, next insertion goes to index 0 if FRONT is not at 0.

keywords.explain: REAR points to the last inserted element's next position (or the slot to insert next), and FRONT points to the element to be deleted next.
keywords.example: Initially both are 0. After one insertion REAR becomes 1; after one deletion FRONT becomes 1.

keywords.explain: Using (index + 1) mod n ensures the index wraps around from n-1 back to 0, implementing circular behavior.
keywords.example: (4+1) mod 5 = 0, so index wraps from 4 back to 0.

understand.visual_alt: Array of 5 slots (indices 0-4) used as a circular queue. Empty state: FRONT=REAR=0. Full state (4 elements): REAR is at index 4, (REAR+1) mod 5 = 0 = FRONT.

given.aim: Find the correct conditions for 'queue full' and 'queue empty' in a circular queue of capacity (n-1) implemented in an array of n elements.

terms.example: n=5 means array indices 0 to 4, queue can hold 4 elements
terms.connects: Determines the modulo base for wrap-around

terms.meaning: Index variable pointing to the position where the next element will be inserted
terms.example: Initially 0; after inserting one element, REAR = (0+1) mod n = 1
terms.connects: Advances by (REAR+1) mod n after each insertion

terms.meaning: Index variable pointing to the position from which the next element will be deleted
terms.example: Initially 0; after deleting one element, FRONT = (FRONT+1) mod n
terms.connects: Advances by (FRONT+1) mod n after each deletion

terms.meaning: Capacity is (n-1) instead of n; one slot is kept empty to differentiate full from empty
terms.example: With n=5, at most 4 elements can be stored simultaneously
terms.connects: Leads to the full condition: (REAR+1) mod n == FRONT

given.plan: Initially REAR = FRONT = 0. When empty, no elements have been inserted so REAR == FRONT. When full, REAR has advanced to just before FRONT in the circular order, meaning (REAR+1) mod n == FRONT. This leaves one slot empty intentionally.

steps.title: Determine the empty condition
steps.apply: Initially both REAR and FRONT are 0. Since REAR always moves ahead of FRONT (insertions advance REAR, deletions advance FRONT), the queue becomes empty again whenever REAR catches up to FRONT — i.e., REAR == FRONT. This is exactly the initial condition and is restored each time all elements are dequeued.
steps.note: REAR == FRONT means no elements lie between FRONT and REAR in the circular order.

steps.title: Determine the full condition
steps.apply: The queue holds (n-1) elements at most. REAR advances circularly. When REAR is at position such that the very next position (circularly) equals FRONT, it means adding one more element would make REAR == FRONT which is the empty condition — ambiguous. So we stop one step before: full when (REAR+1) mod n == FRONT. Example: n=5, FRONT=0, after 4 insertions REAR=4, (4+1) mod 5 = 0 = FRONT, so queue is full.
steps.note: One slot is sacrificed to avoid the ambiguity between full and empty states.

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.apply: Option A states: full when (REAR+1) mod n == FRONT, empty when REAR == FRONT. This matches our derived conditions exactly.
steps.note: Option D reverses FRONT and REAR in the full condition, which would be incorrect.

solution.result: Option A: full when (REAR+1) mod n == FRONT, empty when REAR == FRONT.

questions.formula_note: Circular queue index arithmetic: next position = (index+1) mod n. One slot is wasted to distinguish full (REAR+1 mod n == FRONT) from empty (REAR == FRONT).

questions.to_find: Worst case time complexity of n queue operations (Enqueue, Dequeue, MultiDequeue) on an initially empty queue

understand.plain: We have a queue supporting three operations: Enqueue (add one element), Dequeue (remove one element), and MultiDequeue (remove up to k elements). We perform a sequence of n such operations starting from an empty queue. The question asks for the worst-case time complexity of the entire sequence. The key insight is that you cannot dequeue more elements than you have enqueued, so the total number of individual dequeue operations is bounded by the total number of enqueue operations.

keywords.explain: A technique to analyze the average cost per operation over a sequence of operations, even when individual operations may vary in cost.
keywords.example: Even if one MultiDequeue removes many elements, it cannot remove elements that were never enqueued.

keywords.explain: An operation that dequeues up to k elements from the queue in one call, stopping earlier if the queue becomes empty.
keywords.example: If k=5 and queue has 3 elements, MultiDequeue removes all 3 elements.

understand.visual_alt: A sequence of n operations on an initially empty queue. Each Enqueue adds one element; Dequeue and MultiDequeue remove elements. Total elements removed cannot exceed total elements added.

given.aim: Find the worst case time complexity of n queue operations (Enqueue/Dequeue/MultiDequeue) on an initially empty queue.

terms.meaning: Total number of queue operations in the sequence
terms.example: n=10 could mean 5 Enqueues, 2 Dequeues, and 3 MultiDequeues
terms.connects: Determines the upper bound on total work done

terms.meaning: Global parameter — maximum number of elements MultiDequeue attempts to remove
terms.example: k=100 means MultiDequeue can remove at most 100 elements per call
terms.connects: Even with large k, the actual removals are bounded by what was enqueued

terms.meaning: The queue has 0 elements at the start of the operation sequence
terms.example: No Dequeue or MultiDequeue can remove anything until at least one Enqueue is done
terms.connects: Limits total dequeues to at most the total number of enqueues performed

given.plan: Use amortized analysis (aggregate method). In n operations, at most n Enqueue operations can occur. Each element can be dequeued at most once (by either Dequeue or MultiDequeue). Therefore, the total number of individual Dequeue actions across all operations is at most n. Total work is O(n), giving Theta(n) in the worst case.

steps.title: Count total Enqueue operations
steps.apply: In a sequence of n operations, the number of Enqueue calls is at most n (if every operation is Enqueue). So at most n elements are ever added to the queue.
steps.note: This bounds the total number of elements that can ever be dequeued.

steps.title: Count total actual Dequeue actions
steps.formula_raw: Total dequeue actions <= total enqueues <= n
steps.apply: Each element can be dequeued at most once. MultiDequeue(Q) dequeues one element per iteration of its while loop, but the loop terminates when the queue is empty. Since at most n elements were ever enqueued, the total number of individual dequeue actions across ALL MultiDequeue (and Dequeue) calls in the entire sequence is at most n.
steps.note: k only limits one call of MultiDequeue; it does not multiply the total work done across all n operations.

steps.title: Compute total time complexity
steps.apply: Total work = (cost of all Enqueue operations) + (cost of all Dequeue/MultiDequeue actual dequeues) <= n + n = 2n = O(n). Since any sequence of n operations takes at least Omega(n) time, the complexity is Theta(n).
steps.note: This is an aggregate amortized argument: even though one MultiDequeue might look expensive, the queue cannot have more elements than were enqueued.

questions.formula_note: Aggregate amortized analysis: total dequeue work across all operations is bounded by total enqueue work, both at most n. Hence Theta(n) for n operations.

questions.question:
A queue is implemented using an array such that ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE operations are performed efficiently. Which one of the following statements is CORRECT (n refers to the number of items in the queue)?

A. Both operations can be performed in O(1) time.
B. At most one operation can be performed in O(1) time but the worst case time for the other operation will be Omega(n).
C. The worst case time complexity for both operations will be Omega(n).
D. Worst case time for at least one of the operations will be Omega(log n).
questions.answer: A. Both operations can be performed in O(1) time.
questions.to_find: Time complexity of ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE when a queue is implemented with an array for efficiency

understand.plain: A queue can be implemented using an array in multiple ways. A naive approach (shifting elements on every dequeue) makes one operation O(n). However, using a circular array with two pointers (front and rear), both ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE can be done in O(1) time. The question asks which statement about time complexity is correct for an efficient array-based implementation.

keywords.explain: An array-based queue where front and rear pointers wrap around using modulo arithmetic, avoiding the need to shift elements.
keywords.example: With FRONT and REAR both starting at 0, ENQUEUE advances REAR and DEQUEUE advances FRONT, each in O(1).

keywords.explain: With a circular array, ENQUEUE simply places element at arr[REAR] and advances REAR; DEQUEUE returns arr[FRONT] and advances FRONT — both O(1).
keywords.example: No loop needed, just index arithmetic and one assignment.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 420 160' width='420' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='50' width='60' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='40' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>F</text><rect x='70' y='50' width='60' height='40' class='ok'/><text x='100' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><rect x='130' y='50' width='60' height='40' class='ok'/><text x='160' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B</text><rect x='190' y='50' width='60' height='40' class='ok'/><text x='220' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C</text><rect x='250' y='50' width='60' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='280' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R</text><rect x='310' y='50' width='60' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='340' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl mut'>_</text><text x='40' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>FRONT</text><text x='280' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>REAR</text><text x='10' y='120' class='sub ink'>DEQUEUE: return arr[FRONT]; FRONT = (FRONT+1) mod n — O(1)</text><text x='10' y='140' class='sub ink'>ENQUEUE: arr[REAR] = x; REAR = (REAR+1) mod n — O(1)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Circular array with FRONT and REAR pointers. DEQUEUE reads from FRONT position and advances FRONT in O(1). ENQUEUE writes at REAR position and advances REAR in O(1).

given.aim: Determine the time complexity of ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE for an efficiently implemented array-based queue.

terms.meaning: Number of items currently in the queue
terms.example: If 5 elements are in the queue, n=5
terms.connects: We want to know if operations depend on n or are constant

terms.meaning: Operation to add an element at the rear of the queue
terms.example: In circular array: arr[REAR] = x; REAR = (REAR+1) mod capacity
terms.connects: Takes O(1) with circular array — no shifting needed

terms.meaning: Operation to remove the element from the front of the queue
terms.example: In circular array: x = arr[FRONT]; FRONT = (FRONT+1) mod capacity
terms.connects: Takes O(1) with circular array — no shifting of remaining elements needed

given.plan: With a circular array implementation using two pointers FRONT and REAR, both ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE perform only index arithmetic and a single array access. Neither operation loops over elements, giving O(1) = Theta(1) for both.

steps.title: Analyse naive vs. efficient array implementation
steps.apply: A naive implementation where DEQUEUE shifts all elements left is O(n). But the question specifies the queue is implemented so that ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE are performed EFFICIENTLY. The standard efficient method is a circular array with FRONT and REAR pointers.
steps.note: The keyword 'efficiently' signals we should use the circular array approach, not naive shifting.

steps.title: Verify O(1) for ENQUEUE
steps.apply: ENQUEUE places the new element at arr[REAR] and increments REAR circularly. This is exactly 2 operations regardless of how many elements are in the queue. Time = O(1).
steps.note: If the underlying array is full, we would need to resize (amortized O(1) for dynamic arrays), but for a fixed-capacity queue, it is strictly O(1).

steps.title: Verify O(1) for DEQUEUE
steps.apply: DEQUEUE reads the element at arr[FRONT] and increments FRONT circularly. This is exactly 2 operations regardless of n. Time = O(1).
steps.note: No elements are moved or shifted; only the FRONT index changes.

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: Both operations are O(1). Option A states 'Both operations can be performed in O(1) time' — this is correct. Options B, C, D all claim at least one operation is Omega(n) or Omega(log n), which is incorrect for a circular array implementation.

solution.result: Option A: Both ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE can be performed in O(1) time using a circular array implementation.

questions.formula_note: Circular array queue with FRONT and REAR pointers: both ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE perform only constant-time index arithmetic, giving O(1) per operation.
